Adding OEWS Wage and Employment Context

Source: vignettes/oews-wage-context.Rmd
oews-wage-context.Rmd
library(onet2r)
library(dplyr)
#> 
#> Attaching package: 'dplyr'
#> The following objects are masked from 'package:stats':
#> 
#>     filter, lag
#> The following objects are masked from 'package:base':
#> 
#>     intersect, setdiff, setequal, union

O*NET tells you what occupations do: their tasks, abilities, skills, knowledge, and work contexts. The Bureau of Labor Statistics Occupational Employment and Wage Statistics (OEWS) program tells you how large those occupations are in the labor market and what they pay. Joining the two lets you move from an occupation-level descriptor table to a labor-market-weighted question.

This walkthrough asks: among occupations in a small O*NET ability panel, which ability ratings matter most once we account for national employment and wages? The OEWS file is bundled with the package so the article runs without network access. In production, call onet_oews_national() without path to download the public BLS file.

sample_oews <- system.file(
  "extdata",
  "oews-national-sample.csv",
  package = "onet2r"
)

archive_303 <- system.file(
  "extdata",
  "onet-mini",
  "db_30_3_text",
  package = "onet2r"
)

Read OEWS Estimates

onet_oews_national() normalizes BLS OEWS files into snake_case columns and parses formatted employment and wage fields into numeric values.

oews <- onet_oews_national(year = 2024, path = sample_oews)
oews
#> # A tibble: 3 × 7
#>    year oews_type occ_code occ_title           tot_emp a_median h_median
#>   <int> <chr>     <chr>    <chr>                 <dbl>    <dbl>    <dbl>
#> 1  2024 national  15-1252  Software Developers 1847900   133080     64.0
#> 2  2024 national  29-1141  Registered Nurses   3175400    93070     44.8
#> 3  2024 national  11-1011  Chief Executives     211230   206680     99.4

Key columns for most O*NET joins are occ_code, tot_emp, and a_median.

oews |>
  select(occ_code, occ_title, tot_emp, a_median, h_median)
#> # A tibble: 3 × 5
#>   occ_code occ_title           tot_emp a_median h_median
#>   <chr>    <chr>                 <dbl>    <dbl>    <dbl>
#> 1 15-1252  Software Developers 1847900   133080     64.0
#> 2 29-1141  Registered Nurses   3175400    93070     44.8
#> 3 11-1011  Chief Executives     211230   206680     99.4

Get O*NET Occupations from an Actual Package Function

The occupation list below comes from onet_archive_read(), not a hand-built example table. It reads a local archive-format O*NET Abilities table and then keeps one row per occupation.

abilities <- onet_archive_read(
  "30.3",
  "Abilities",
  path = archive_303,
  release_date = "2026-05-01"
)

occupations <- abilities |>
  distinct(code = onet_soc_code, title)

occupations
#> # A tibble: 4 × 2
#>   code       title                                         
#>   <chr>      <chr>                                         
#> 1 15-1252.00 Software Developers                           
#> 2 29-1141.00 Registered Nurses                             
#> 3 11-1011.00 Chief Executives                              
#> 4 41-1011.00 First-Line Supervisors of Retail Sales Workers

Join OEWS to O*NET Occupation Codes

O*NET-SOC codes commonly include a detail suffix such as .00. OEWS uses six-digit SOC codes. onet_join_oews() creates a normalized soc_code and joins on that value.

occupation_wages <- onet_join_oews(occupations, oews = oews)

occupation_wages |>
  select(code, title, soc_code, tot_emp, a_median, h_median)
#> # A tibble: 4 × 6
#>   code       title                            soc_code tot_emp a_median h_median
#>   <chr>      <chr>                            <chr>      <dbl>    <dbl>    <dbl>
#> 1 15-1252.00 Software Developers              15-1252  1847900   133080     64.0
#> 2 29-1141.00 Registered Nurses                29-1141  3175400    93070     44.8
#> 3 11-1011.00 Chief Executives                 11-1011   211230   206680     99.4
#> 4 41-1011.00 First-Line Supervisors of Retai… 41-1011       NA       NA     NA

The join also tells you where wage context is missing. Missing rows usually mean you need a different OEWS geography, a different year, or a crosswalk between classification vintages.

occupation_wages |>
  transmute(
    code,
    title,
    has_oews_match = !is.na(tot_emp)
  )
#> # A tibble: 4 × 3
#>   code       title                                          has_oews_match
#>   <chr>      <chr>                                          <lgl>         
#> 1 15-1252.00 Software Developers                            TRUE          
#> 2 29-1141.00 Registered Nurses                              TRUE          
#> 3 11-1011.00 Chief Executives                               TRUE          
#> 4 41-1011.00 First-Line Supervisors of Retail Sales Workers FALSE

Compute Employment-Weighted O*NET Summaries

If you average ability scores across occupations without weights, a niche occupation counts the same as an occupation employing millions of workers. onet_weighted_summary() uses OEWS employment, and optionally wage, to summarize O*NET descriptor rows at the labor-market level.

weighted_abilities <- onet_weighted_summary(
  abilities,
  group = c("element_id", "element_name"),
  value = "data_value",
  occupation_code = "onet_soc_code",
  oews = oews
)

weighted_abilities
#> # A tibble: 2 × 7
#>   element_id element_name     n_records n_occupations total_weight weighted_mean
#>   <chr>      <chr>                <int>         <int>        <dbl>         <dbl>
#> 1 1.A.1.a.1  Oral Comprehens…         3             3      5234530          4.57
#> 2 1.A.1.b.1  Problem Sensiti…         3             3      5234530          4.73
#> # ℹ 1 more variable: wage_weighted_mean <dbl>

In this example, n_occupations is the number of O*NET occupations with a matching OEWS estimate for each ability row. total_weight is the summed OEWS employment used in the weighted mean.

Prioritize Occupation-Level Rows

A practical analyst often wants to know where a descriptor is both high and attached to a large workforce. The next table joins ability rows to OEWS and sorts by employment-weighted ability contribution.

ability_context <- abilities |>
  onet_join_oews(oews = oews, by = "onet_soc_code") |>
  mutate(weighted_value = data_value * tot_emp) |>
  select(
    onet_soc_code,
    title,
    element_name,
    data_value,
    tot_emp,
    a_median,
    weighted_value
  ) |>
  arrange(desc(weighted_value))

head(ability_context, 10)
#> # A tibble: 7 × 7
#>   onet_soc_code title    element_name data_value tot_emp a_median weighted_value
#>   <chr>         <chr>    <chr>             <dbl>   <dbl>    <dbl>          <dbl>
#> 1 29-1141.00    Registe… Problem Sen…       4.9  3175400    93070      15559460 
#> 2 29-1141.00    Registe… Oral Compre…       4.71 3175400    93070      14956134 
#> 3 15-1252.00    Softwar… Problem Sen…       4.5  1847900   133080       8315550 
#> 4 15-1252.00    Softwar… Oral Compre…       4.35 1847900   133080       8038365 
#> 5 11-1011.00    Chief E… Oral Compre…       4.5   211230   206680        950535 
#> 6 11-1011.00    Chief E… Problem Sen…       4.22  211230   206680        891391.
#> 7 41-1011.00    First-L… Oral Compre…       4.15      NA       NA            NA

This table is not a causal estimate. It is a screening tool. It helps answer questions such as: “Which O*NET ability ratings are attached to the most workers?” or “Which occupations should we inspect first when designing training or workforce-transition analyses?”

PUMS Employment Weights

If your workflow uses ACS or CPS microdata instead of OEWS employment counts, onet_pums_employment_weights() converts person-level records into SOC-level weights. The tiny input below is intentionally minimal: in a real analysis, SOCP and PWGTP would usually come from tidycensus::get_pums().

pums <- tibble::tibble(
  SOCP = c("151252", "151252", "291141"),
  PWGTP = c(120, 80, 200)
)

onet_pums_employment_weights(pums)
#> # A tibble: 2 × 3
#>   soc_code employment records
#>   <chr>         <dbl>   <int>
#> 1 15-1252         200       2
#> 2 29-1141         200       1

Use OEWS when you want official occupation employment and wage estimates. Use PUMS-derived weights when your analysis needs custom populations, geographies, or demographic slices that OEWS does not publish directly.

On this page
